app.heroSubtitle: Batteries hit $78/kWh. But 95% of the world's cars still burn oil. Why?
app.act1Subtitle: Batteries are $78/kWh — so why are there still 1.4 billion ICEVs on the road?
app.act2Subtitle: Three continents, three strategies — but infrastructure is the real track where the race is won or lost
app.act3Subtitle: Regression analysis reveals which policies actually move the needle — and which are just theater
app.act4Subtitle: Environmental benefits are real but uneven — the grid is the final variable
app.footerText: An interactive data documentation project exploring the transition from ICEVs to EVs. Through a four-chapter causal chain — cost, scale, policy, carbon — it argues that electrification is not a question of "if" but "how fast" and "who leads."
app.footerMethod1: TCO Model: Annual cumulative depreciation + city-weighted energy costs
app.footerMethod2: Battery Forecast: Triple model comparison (Bounded Exponential R²=0.97, Wright's Law 17.4% doubling rate)
app.footerMethod3: Policy Sandbox: OLS multiple linear regression (R²=0.65, n=400)
app.footerMethod4: Carbon Race: Woody 2026 full lifecycle parameters
app.footerBottom: Built with Vue 3 + ECharts + D3.js | Data as of April 2026
app.endBody1: In 1991, batteries cost $9,210/kWh. In 2024, $78. This isn't a cliff — it's a decoupling. The gravity of cost has been overcome. What remains is not a technology problem but a question of political will, infrastructure investment, and grid decarbonization. Each chapter's data answers the same puzzle: if batteries are cheap enough, why hasn't the world switched? The answer isn't that consumers don't want EVs — it's that chargers are missing, policies are thin, and grids are dirty. But these variables are changing fast.
app.endBody2: Every country, every individual, is choosing where they stand on this S-curve. The data speaks. The direction is clear.
app.endResearchQuestion: This visualization doesn't directly answer "will EVs replace ICEVs?" — the data already answers that. It pursues a deeper puzzle: if batteries are already cheap enough ($78/kWh), why do 95% of the world's cars still burn oil? Four chapters form a causal chain that peels back this contradiction layer by layer — cost decoupling ≠ market decoupling (Ch1), scale expansion is wildly uneven (Ch2), policy effectiveness varies by country (Ch3), and carbon payoff depends on the grid (Ch4). Readers arrive at conclusions themselves through the data, rather than being told the answer upfront.
app.endResearchDesign: Narrative Architecture: The four-chapter causal chain follows a "pose contradiction → answer with data → introduce next contradiction" structure. Each chapter opens with a question rather than a conclusion. This design mirrors the research's core finding — no single silver bullet drives EV transition; it requires systemic coordination of cost, infrastructure, policy, and grid.||Technical Implementation: Chapter 1's battery price analysis uses three regression models (Exponential Decay R²=0.963, Bounded Exponential R²=0.972 with $50/kWh material cost floor, Experience Curve at 17.4%/doubling), paired with TCO modeling that introduces the ICEV "moat" narrative (used car price resilience, gas station sunk costs). Chapter 2 uses IEA 2025 multi-regional data (16K rows) and 5-country charging infrastructure comparison, using Norway's counterexample (12K chargers → 90% share) to show charger count ≠ EV success. Chapter 3's OLS regression (R²=0.65, n=400) quantifies each policy factor's contribution, with every slider accompanied by paper-based tooltip annotations explaining the mechanism. Chapter 4 uses Woody 2026 lifecycle parameters across 9 grid scenarios, framing the analysis as "same car, different carbon fate" to address the most common objection.||Interaction Principle: All charts support hover, filtering, parameter adjustment, and model comparison — letting users discover conclusions from data rather than being persuaded by them. Collapsible layers progress by depth: always-visible layer shows core findings, fold 1 provides interactive verification, fold 2 presents methodology and paper evidence.
app.endResearchSources: [Datasets]||IEA Global EV Outlook 2025 — GEVO_EV_2025_main.csv (16,437 rows): Global EV sales, stock, market share, charging stations, subsidies for 25 countries, 2010-2024 historical + STEPS 2025-2030 projection||IEA Global EV Outlook 2025 — EV_sales_by_country.csv (63 rows), EV_sales_by_macro_region.csv (12 rows), other_parameters.csv (12 rows)||Our World in Data — owid_lithium_ion_battery_cell_price.csv: Lithium-ion cell price 1991-2024 (34 data points)||Our World in Data — owid_ev_sales_share.csv, owid_bev_share_of_new_ev.csv, owid_plugin_bev_sales_share.csv: Global EV sales share||Our World in Data — owid_co2_emissions_by_fuel.csv: CO₂ emissions by fuel type, 1800-2024||Our World in Data — owid_global_energy_substitution.csv: Global energy substitution, 1800-2024||Our World in Data — owid_lead_petrol_ban.csv: Leaded petrol ban timeline (129 countries)||BloombergNEF — battery_price.csv: Battery pack price 2010-2024 + two projection scenarios 2025-2030||Washington State Dept. of Transportation — us_wa_ev_population_data.csv (150,482 rows): EV registration data||World Bank Open Data — GDP per capita, urbanization rates||China Charging Alliance — china_charging_stations.csv: Public chargers (AC/DC) 2010-2025||Norway/Sweden/Netherlands/Germany charging data — 2011-2019 national statistics||China Passenger Car Association — China EV market structure by segment (A00-C class)||TCO Analysis Dataset — vehicle_class_definition.csv, tco_summary_all_classes.csv, specs_*.csv, comparison_*.csv (A00/A/B/C class BEV vs ICEV)||Emisia/ICCT — Emission regulation database (25-country regulation scores)||[References]||[1] Woody M, Yin S, Green A, et al. app.endDevOverviewText: This project was developed entirely by one person using AI-assisted coding, with no third-party templates or scaffolding tools. The tech stack is Vue 3 Composition API + ECharts 6 + D3.js — all chart components are hand-written, not wrapped from existing chart libraries. Development took approximately two weeks.
app.endDevTimelineText:
Days 1-2 · Topic Selection & Data Collection: Chose the EV transition theme, collected raw datasets from IEA 2025, OWID, BloombergNEF (170K+ rows of CSV combined), and read 14 related papers to establish the causal framework. The biggest challenge here was identifying the key variables that could support a coherent causal chain from the overwhelming volume of data.

Days 3-4 · Data Preprocessing Scripts: Built a Node.js data cleaning pipeline (scripts/processors/), including IEA 16K-row extraction, battery price triple-model fitting (Exponential Decay / Bounded Exponential / Experience Curve), 5-country charging station standardization, and energy transition data processing. Battery price regression was the most time-consuming — requiring Levenberg-Marquardt nonlinear fitting and Bootstrap confidence intervals.

Days 5-8 · Frontend Component Development: Built the Vue 3 project structure from scratch, implementing a custom i18n system, responsive theme switching, and scroll-driven animation system. Developed 7 chart components one by one (battery price trend, TCO calculator, EV growth area chart, charging infrastructure comparison, policy heatmap, policy sandbox, carbon race), each requiring careful integration of ECharts with Vue's reactivity system.

Days 9-10 · Narrative Restructuring: Based on user feedback, shifted the narrative from "declaring victory" to "building suspense" — renamed Ch1 from "Cost Cliff" to "Cost Decoupling," changed each chapter opening to a question format, and introduced the ICEV "moat" narrative. This round modified 200+ lines of i18n text across both Chinese and English.

Days 11-14 · Polish & Bug Fixes: Fixed chart interaction issues (EV growth x-axis ordering, charging station scale, tooltip i18n), enhanced the policy sandbox (added paper-based tooltip annotations for each factor), rewrote grid integration section with proper citations, compiled the full reference list, and adjusted the end cover layout. Final build output: ~350KB JS + 26KB CSS gzipped.
app.endDevReflectionText: The biggest design challenge of this project was not technical but narrative — how to make data tell a story with tension rather than just stacking numbers. The initial version gave away "EVs have already won" in Chapter 1, turning the remaining three chapters into epilogues of a known ending. The restructuring changed each chapter to pose a contradiction, answer it with data, then introduce the next contradiction — letting readers form their own conclusions as they scroll. On the technical side, ECharts 6 category axis sorting, dual-axis tooltip alignment, and large-dataset rendering optimization all took more time than expected. If I were to start over, I would invest more time in the data preprocessing phase — many chart bugs later traced back to inconsistent data formats.

scenario.emptyHint: Use the "Save" button in any chapter to add scenarios for comparison.

act1.title: Chapter 1: Battery & TCO Data Sources
act1.sources: Our World in Data — Lithium-ion battery cell price 1991-2024 (34 data points)||BloombergNEF Annual Battery Price Survey — 2010-2024 pack prices||IEA Global EV Data Explorer 2025 — EV stock data for Experience Curve||Washington State EV Registration Data — actual registrations validation||World Bank Open Data — GDP per capita, urbanization rates||Consumer Reports & Kelley Blue Book — vehicle MSRP and depreciation rates||Woody et al. 2026 (Environ. Res. Lett. 21 024022) — used vehicle TCO analysis||BEUC 2025 — European EV price parity timeline||Baek et al. 2026 (eTransportation) — long-term TCO projections||Nykvist & Nilsson 2015 (Nature Climate Change) — battery cost decline systematic review||Ziegler & Trancik 2021 (Energy Environ. Sci.) — lithium-ion battery improvement rates and cost decline
act1.methodology: Battery price triple model: (1) Exponential Decay P(t)=a·e^(-bt), R²=0.963; (2) Bounded Exponential P(t)=50+a·e^(-bt) with $50/kWh material cost floor, R²=0.972 (best fit by AIC); (3) Experience Curve P(stock)=a·stock^b using IEA cumulative EV stock, 17.4% cost reduction per doubling (consistent with Ziegler & Trancik 2021's finding of 20.4% learning rate). All models use log-linear least squares; Bounded Exponential uses Levenberg-Marquardt nonlinear fitting. TCO model uses exponential depreciation (1-r)^t where r is annual rate (ICEV 10-12%, BEV 13-15%). TCO defaults are US-market based: electricity $0.15/kWh, subsidy $7,500 (federal tax credit), 15,000 miles/year.
act1.limitations: Battery price fits assume continuation of historical trends — raw material shocks (e.g. 2022 lithium spike) can cause temporary reversals. Experience Curve uses EV stock as proxy for cumulative battery production. TCO values are US-market representative, not model-specific. Used vehicle TCO findings (Woody 2026) are based on US market conditions.

act2.title: Chapter 2: Scale & Infrastructure Data Sources
act2.sources: IEA Global EV Outlook 2025 (GEVO_EV_2025_main.csv, 16,437 rows) — global EV sales/stock by region||China Charging Infrastructure data — 2010-2025 public charger counts (AC/DC)||Norway/Sweden/Netherlands/Germany charging data — 2011-2019 national statistics||China Passenger Car Association — China EV market structure by segment (A00-C)||Noll et al. 2026 (Nature Energy) — African BEV viability with solar off-grid charging||Figenbaum 2017 (Environ. Innov. Soc. Transit.) — Norway EV policy case study
act2.methodology: IEA data extracted for Cars mode, merging BEV+PHEV, separated into Historical (2010-2024) and STEPS projection (2025-2030). CAGR calculated by region. Charging data standardized across 5 countries with heterogeneous CSV formats. China market structure shows annual sales share by vehicle class (A00-C).
act2.limitations: Charging data coverage varies: China has data through 2025, European countries only through 2019. BEV sales figures for China post-2020 are IEA estimates. Market class boundaries are approximate — some models span categories.

act3.title: Chapter 3: Policy & Market Penetration Data Sources
act3.sources: IEA Global EV Data Explorer 2025 — 25-country EV sales/market share/charging stations/subsidies||World Bank Open Data — GDP per capita, urbanization rates||Emisia/ICCT emission regulation database — regulation scores||Woodley et al. 2024 (Nature Communications) — mineral supply chain analysis||Noll et al. 2026 (Nature Energy) — financing as key policy lever
act3.methodology: OLS multiple linear regression: dependent variable is EV market share, independent variables are subsidy amount, log(charging stations+1), fuel price, electricity price, regulation score, log(GDP+1). Log transforms handle skewed distributions. Sensitivity analysis measures partial R² drop from removing each variable. The regression framework is consistent with Shevchenko (2024)'s finding of r=0.667 correlation between charging infrastructure and EV registrations, which also cites Yu, Li & Tong (2016) on indirect network effects.
act3.limitations: Linear regression assumes linear relationships, potentially underestimating extreme policies (e.g., Norway). Market share has [0, 100] bounds, model may predict out-of-range values. Some 2024-2025 data points are extrapolated/estimated. Mineral supply analysis (Woodley 2024) is based on known reserves — new discoveries could change projections.

act4.title: Chapter 4: Lifecycle Carbon Emissions Data Sources
act4.sources: Woody et al. 2026 (Environ. Res. Lett. 21 024022) — lifecycle emission parameters||IEA Electricity Information 2025 — country grid CO₂ intensity||IPCC AR6 — fuel emission factor (2.31 kg CO₂/L gasoline)||Our World in Data — global energy substitution 1800-2024||Our World in Data — leaded petrol ban timeline (129 countries)||Emissions Transition Structure 2026 — embodied vs operational emissions trends||Ember 2025 — Global Electricity Review (clean power reached 40.9% of global generation)
act4.methodology: BEV annual emissions = vehicle energy consumption × grid CO₂ intensity. ICEV annual emissions = fuel consumption × fuel emission factor. Manufacturing emissions include body + battery manufacturing (extra for BEV). Crossover year calculated via linear interpolation. Grid CO₂ linearly scales BEV annual emissions relative to US baseline (0.39 kg/kWh). Emission parameters are based on Woody et al. 2026's BEV vs ICEV lifecycle analysis covering 5 vehicle types across 17 US cities. Energy transition data covers 1800-2024 with 7 energy types.
act4.limitations: Manufacturing emission data are literature averages, not specific to manufacturers or battery chemistry. Grid CO₂ uses national averages, ignoring regional/time variation. Does not include carbon credits from battery recycling or second-life applications. 15-year ownership is an assumed upper bound.

act1.insightTitle: Batteries hit $78/kWh — so why do EVs still only account for 18% of new car sales?
act1.insightBody: In 2024, lithium-ion battery costs fell to $78/kWh — below the $100/kWh parity threshold. Two well-fitted models confirm this downward trend is robustⓘ. Yet EVs still account for only 18% of global new car salesⓘ. The cost has decoupled — but the market hasn't caught up. Why?
act1.narrativeP1: The battery is the single most expensive component in an EV, accounting for 30-40% of vehicle cost. Over 34 years, battery prices fell from $9,210 to $78/kWhⓘ. Our triple-model analysisⓘ shows the curve is shifting from exponential decay to linearly approaching the $50/kWh material cost floor — the room for further battery cost reduction is narrowing. But ICEVs haven't stood still either — a 3-year-old used ICEV still costs less upfront than a comparable new BEV, and 120,000+ gas stations worldwide represent trillions in sunk assets. Cost has decoupled, but ICEVs retain economic inertia.
act1.narrativeP2: Stretch the timeline to 5-7 years, however, and the picture flips. Woody et al. 2026 analyzed over 260,000 used vehicle listings across 17 US cities and 5 vehicle classes, finding that a 3-year-old used BEV saves approximately $13,000 over 7 years vs a new ICEVⓘ. Nykvist & Nilsson (2015)'s systematic review also found industry-wide battery cost declines of ~14% annually, consistent with our model. Even in infrastructure-poor Africa, Noll et al. 2026 found that solar off-grid BEVs will achieve cost advantages before 2040 — the bottleneck is financing, not technology. Use the calculator below to verify these numbers yourself.
act1.bridgeP: The math works — but only for people with chargers. Of the world's 1.4 billion ICEVs, most have never seen a public charging point. How do cheap batteries reach African dirt roads and American apartment parking lots?

act2.insightTitle: One country has 60% — the other 190 share the remaining 40%. What went wrong?
act2.insightBody: In 2024, China alone accounted for over 60% of global EV salesⓘ. But the more telling number is structural: Norway has only 11,889 public chargers yet achieves over 90% EV market share; China has 3.43 million chargers but only 48% share. Raw charger counts don't determine EV adoption — policy design, industrial ecosystems, and consumer incentives working in concert is what matters. Half of the world's top 10 auto markets still have EV penetration below 5%. Noll et al. 2026 shows that even in Africa, solar off-grid charging makes BEVs viable before 2040 — but financing, not technology, is the bottleneck.
act2.narrativeP1: According to the IEA Global EV Outlook 2025, global EV sales grew from 120,000 in 2012 to approximately 17 million in 2024ⓘ. But this growth is wildly uneven. Norway achieved >90% penetration with just 12,000 chargers — Figenbaum (2017) shows the secret is tax exemptions built up since 1990ⓘ that make EVs cheaper to buy than ICEVs. China took a different path: 3.43 million chargers + subsidies + vertically integrated supply chains. Two paths, one conclusion — no single factor explains EV expansion; it requires systemic coordination of policy, infrastructure, and industry.
act2.narrativeP2: Absolute sales tell only half the story. Growth rates paint an even sharper picture: China's EV sales surged ~160% YoY in 2021, and has added millions of vehicles every year since. Europe experienced a boom in 2020 (+120%), but growth slowed noticeably in 2023-2024. India, though starting from a small base, has consistently exceeded 100% annual growthⓘ. The growth divergence reveals three distinct strategies: China scales through supply chains, Europe pulls demand through policy, and India enters through low-cost volume.
act2.narrativeP3: But cost declines mean nothing without infrastructure. China grew its public charger network from 140K in 2016 to 2.23M in 2024, maintaining a charger-to-BEV-sales ratio of 57%–84% — meaning roughly 0.6–0.8 public chargers per BEV sold each year. Globally, countries where charger density reaches a critical threshold consistently see EV penetration above 15%. Norway's example also reminds us: more chargers isn't always better — the key is policy that gives consumers the motivation and means to choose EVs.
act2.bridgeP: Infrastructure determines speed. But who pays for these chargers? The answer isn't the market — it's government. The question is: which policies actually work, and which are just political theater?
act2.batteryChartDesc: 1991-2024 actual + three models with 95% confidence bands
act2.raceChartDesc: Year-over-year growth rate (%) — hover for details
act2.brandChartDesc: 10 brands across 10 countries — bubble size = battery capacity
act2.chargingChartDesc: 5 countries: public charger growth and charger-to-BEV ratio

act3.insightTitle: Norway 90%, Brazil 2% — same planet, different policies. Where does the gap come from?
act3.insightBody: Multiple regression across 25 countries and 16 years shows: there is no single policy "silver bullet." Norway leads with tax exemptions (EV market share >90%), China overtakes with dual subsidy + infrastructure drive (3.43M chargers), while Brazil and India wait for the price inflection point. But the model quantifies one core finding: based on our OLS regression (R²=0.65, n=400)ⓘ, for every standard deviation increase in charging infrastructure, EV penetration rises ~6.5 percentage points. And Woodley et al. 2024 reveals the policy boundary: known lithium/cobalt supplies can only support 50% of projected BEV demand.
act3.narrativeP1: 25 countries, 16 years of data, one conclusion: there is no single policy "silver bullet" for electrification. Norway leads with hefty tax exemptions (EV market share >90%), China overtakes with dual subsidy + infrastructure drive (3.43M chargers), while Brazil and India wait for the price inflection point. The regression analysisⓘ quantifies what intuition suggests — infrastructure matters most, subsidies help early but fade, and regulation provides a floor. But policy has limits too: mineral bottlenecks (Woodley 2024 — known lithium/cobalt/nickel supply can only meet 50% of BEV demand) and subsidy diminishing returns above 15% penetration. Policy can accelerate, but it can't create supply from nothing.
act3.narrativeP2: The sandbox below lets you play "policy maker": adjust 6 parameters including subsidies, fuel prices, electricity prices, and regulatory strength, and see how the regression model predicts changes in EV market penetration.
act3.narrativeP3: But policy is not omnipotent. In the regression model, the marginal effect of subsidies diminishes significantly after penetration exceeds 15% — at which point infrastructure and electricity prices become the real bottlenecks. Baek et al. 2026 similarly notes that "further investigation is warranted for BEV charging infrastructure" — the completeness of charging infrastructure directly determines whether policy dividends can be realized.
act3.bridgeP: The economics work, the infrastructure is scaling, and we know which policies are effective. But does all of this actually help the planet — or are we just moving emissions from tailpipes to smokestacks?
act3.heatmapChartDesc: 25 countries ranked by EV market share — click a bar to see country details
act3.sandboxChartDesc: Adjust 6 policy parameters — see predicted EV market share in real-time
act3.sensitivityChartDesc: Which factor has the largest impact on EV adoption?
act3.featureTooltipEvSubsidy: The regression coefficient is negative (-0.00068), but this doesn't mean subsidies are ineffective — it reflects reverse causality: countries with low EV penetration tend to introduce higher subsidies to stimulate the market. Shevchenko's research confirms subsidies work in early markets, but infrastructure investment is 3× more effective than purchase subsidies (Yu, Li & Tong, 2016). BEUC 2025 further notes that subsidies don't directly address low BEV residual values, making them less cost-effective than expected.
act3.featureTooltipChargingStations: One of the strongest positive predictors in the model (coefficient +0.61, log-transformed). Shevchenko found a correlation of r=0.667 between charging stations and EV registrations — charging infrastructure alone explains 44.5% of EV registration variance. The key mechanism is eliminating range anxiety and creating indirect network effects: more chargers → more consumer confidence → more EVs → more charger investment.
act3.featureTooltipFuelPrice: Coefficient +4.67 — fuel price is the single largest-impact variable in the model. Higher fuel prices widen the operating cost advantage of EVs over ICEVs, shortening the TCO payback period. This is one of Norway's hidden advantages: high Nordic fuel prices amplify EV economic appeal.
act3.featureTooltipElectricityPrice: Coefficient -6.44 (the largest negative factor). Electricity price directly determines EV charging costs. BEUC 2025 found that if owners can only use public chargers (without access to low-cost home/work charging), TCO parity is delayed by at least 6 years — public charging increases total costs by 5-16%. Electricity price is the floor of EV economics.
act3.featureTooltipRegulationScore: The regulation score (range 19-96) is a composite index measuring a country's emissions regulatory stringency, including ZEV mandates, fuel economy standards, and ICE phase-out timelines. Coefficient +0.25 — each 1-point increase predicts a 0.25pp rise in EV penetration. Regulation's core effect is on the supply side, not demand: it compels automakers to produce EVs and provides long-term policy certainty for industry investment.
act3.featureTooltipGdpPerCapita: The coefficient is negative (-1.74, log-transformed), which seems counterintuitive — wealthier countries with lower EV penetration? The reason is a control variable effect: the regression already explains most variance through fuel price, charging infrastructure, and regulation. GDP captures the residual — in early data (2010-2018), high-GDP countries like the US and Japan indeed had lower EV penetration than China and the Nordics. GDP is not a driver but a proxy for unexplained variation after other variables are accounted for.
act3.mineralBody: Analysis of mineral supply chains reveals that US and allied nations hold sufficient lithium, cobalt, and nickel resources to support only ~50% of projected BEV manufacturing needs. This constraint has three implications:
act3.mineralImplications: HEVs may serve as a bridge technology in mineral-constrained regions|Recycling and urban mining become critical for supply security|Geopolitical diversification of supply chains is a policy priority
act3.gridTitle: Can the Grid Handle Mass EV Charging?
act3.gridIntro: Mass EV integration is not simply additive load — recent research shows that through smart scheduling and multi-vector coordination, the grid can absorb EV charging demand without significant capacity expansion:
act3.gridSolutions: On the demand side, Yang et al. 2026 propose a closed-loop corrective EV demand response strategy using a composite customer directrix load (CCDL) model to handle EV response uncertainty, improving demand response accuracy by 17.88% in distribution networks while accommodating renewable energy.|On the supply side, Qiao et al. 2026 develop a flexibility-based scheduling model for high-penetration VRE systems, using Copula functions to quantify wind-solar output uncertainty, achieving 3.44% system reliability improvement and ¥56M revenue per 1% load-loss risk reduction.|On the storage side, Zhang et al. 2026 propose bi-level optimization for electricity-heat-hydrogen-ammonia storage with ammonia cycling, enabling efficient energy recycling while reducing both economic costs and carbon emissions.|At the system level, Xu & Abdalla 2026 coordinate electric, thermal, and hydrogen vectors with PV, wind, PEMFC-CHP, battery storage, and PHEV charging, using Monte Carlo simulation for uncertainty — achieving lowest cost (320€), lowest emissions (520 kg CO₂), and zero unmet load.|Together, these studies show that grid expansion is not a prerequisite for mass EV adoption — smart scheduling and multi-vector coordination can absorb charging demand on existing infrastructure.
act3.gridSources: Yang J, Xiang Y, Tang Z, et al. Closed-loop corrective EV demand response with composite customer directrix load for renewable energy accommodation in distribution networks. Renewable Energy, 2026, 256: 124403.|Qiao L, Tan Q, Wen X, et al. Complementary scheduling of hydro-wind-PV-storage systems: Addressing flexibility demand for high-penetration variable renewable energy integration. Renewable Energy, 2026, 257: 124718.|Zhang W, Zhang M, Liu J. Optimal multi-energy storage capacity allocation for electricity-heat-hydrogen-ammonia integrated energy systems with ammonia cycling. Renewable Energy, 2026, 257: 124788.|Xu C, Abdalla A N. Coordinated dispatch of electric, thermal, and hydrogen vectors in renewable-enriched microgrids using constrained Harris Hawks optimization under uncertainty. Renewable Energy, 2026, 256: 124064.

act4.insightTitle: Are EVs really greener? The answer depends on where you plug in
act4.insightBody: Norway's EV carbon advantage is 96%; India's is just 18% — the same car, different carbon fates. Woody et al. 2026 shows battery manufacturing adds ~5-6.5 tonnes CO₂, but even in coal-dominated India, EVs still produce fewer lifetime emissions than comparable ICEVs — confirmed across 5 vehicle types and 17 US citiesⓘ. The advantage is smaller, but the data is clear.
act4.narrativeP1: "But EVs run on electricity — where does that come from?" It's the most common objection, and it deserves a serious answer. Noll et al. 2026's lifecycle analysis across 52 African countriesⓘ found that BEVs with solar off-grid charging achieve negative greenhouse gas abatement costs — meaning they save money and reduce emissions simultaneously. Even in coal-dominated India, EVs still produce fewer lifetime emissions than comparable ICEVsⓘ. These are quantifiable facts — lifecycle emissions data provides the answer.
act4.narrativeP2: The grid is the final variable — and it's getting cleaner, faster than most people expect. In Norway (98% clean electricity), the EV carbon advantage reaches 96%; in India (25% clean), it drops to 18%ⓘ. Baek et al. 2026 identifies grid decarbonization as the core variable amplifying BEV carbon advantages. According to Ember 2025, clean power already accounted for 40.9% of global electricity generation in 2024, with solar growing 29%ⓘ. History offers a precedent: leaded petrol was banned in 129 countries, most within a single decade. Renewable energy is now following a similar S-curve trajectory.
act4.bridgeP: Cost decoupled, scale accelerating, policies effective — but the ultimate decider is the grid. It's getting cleaner. The question is: fast enough?
act4.carbonRaceDesc: Cumulative lifecycle CO₂ emissions — watch the crossover point

act4.energyTransitionChartTitle: Global Energy Mix: 224 Years of Transition
act4.energyTransitionChartDesc: Can the grid clean up fast enough? History says yes.
act4.embodiedIntro: Recent analysis reveals a structural shift in emission composition:
act4.embodiedFindings: Operational emissions share declining from 85% to 60% of total lifecycle|Embodied emissions (manufacturing, materials) nearly doubling in share|Carbon budget at risk of exceeding targets by 2037 under current trajectories|Grid decarbonization alone insufficient — manufacturing efficiency also critical
act4.embodiedSource: Source: Emissions Transition Structure 2026 · Woody et al. 2026 lifecycle parameters
